title: Matrices & Operations
blurb: Number grids and their arithmetic: entries and dimensions, adding and scaling, and the row-times-column multiplication that runs everything.
buildsOn: Componentwise thinking from the vector units — a matrix is columns of vectors.

lessons.title: What Is a Matrix?
lessons.concreteComparison: A shop's weekly sales sheet: rows are products, columns are days, each cell one number. That grid IS a matrix. Mathematics adds an address system — entry $a_{23}$ lives in row 2, column 3 — and, crucially, an arithmetic for whole grids at once.
lessons.objective: Read matrix dimensions and entries, use row-column addressing, and decide when two matrices are equal.
concept: A **matrix** is a rectangular grid of numbers. Its **dimension** is rows × columns — a $2 \times 3$ matrix has 2 rows and 3 columns. Rows first, always: the address $a_{ij}$ means row $i$, column $j$.

Two matrices are **equal** only when they have the same dimensions AND every corresponding entry matches. Equality is entrywise — one mismatch anywhere breaks it, which turns matrix equations into systems of ordinary equations.

Special citizens worth naming now: the **square** matrix (rows = columns), the **zero** matrix $O$ (all zeros — the additive nothing), and the **identity** $I = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ — ones down the main diagonal, the multiplicative nothing you'll meet properly two lessons on.
lessons.keyIdea: A matrix is a rows × columns grid; a_ij = row i, column j; equal means same shape and every entry matching.

facts.title: Addressing
facts.explanation: First index picks the row, second the column.

facts.title: Equality
facts.latex: A = B \iff \text{same shape and } a_{ij} = b_{ij} \; \forall i,j
facts.explanation: Entrywise — a matrix equation is many number equations.

workedExamples.statement: For $A = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & -1 & 3 \\ 0 & 7 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, give the dimension of $A$ and the entries $a_{12}$ and $a_{23}$.
workedExamples.solution: $A$ is $2 \times 3$ (2 rows, 3 columns). $a_{12}$ = row 1, column 2 $= -1$; $a_{23}$ = row 2, column 3 $= 2$.

workedExamples.statement: A shop sells items A and B on Sat/Sun: $S = \begin{pmatrix} 12 & 9 \\ 5 & 8 \end{pmatrix}$ (rows = items, columns = days). Read how many of item B sold on Sunday, and the weekend total of item A.
workedExamples.solution: Item B, Sunday = $s_{22} = 8$. Item A total = $12 + 9 = 21$ — row sums tally an item across days.

commonMistakes.text: Swapping rows and columns in the dimension.
commonMistakes.correction: 2 × 3 means 2 ROWS and 3 columns. 'RC' — rows then columns — is the convention everywhere, including a_ij.

commonMistakes.text: Declaring matrices equal from a few matching entries.
commonMistakes.correction: Equality is total: same shape and EVERY entry matching. One cell off = not equal.

steps.title: A grid with an address system
steps.body: Sales sheets, timetables, pixel blocks — number grids are everywhere. A **matrix** is the mathematical grid: dimension counted rows × columns, and every entry addressed as $a_{ij}$ — row $i$, column $j$, rows always first.

steps.title: Equality is entrywise
steps.body: $A = B$ demands the same shape AND all entries matching — so one matrix equation silently packs many number equations, which is exactly how exams use it: hide an $x$ in one entry, a $y$ in another, and let equality do the interrogation. Meet also the zero matrix $O$ and the identity $I$ (ones on the diagonal) — the grid world's 0 and 1.
beats: equal ⇔ same shape + every entry matches

steps.title: Find the entry
steps.explanation: Row 2, column 1: the entry $4$. ($a_{12} = 2$ is its transpose-twin — the classic mix-up.)

steps.title: Same or not?
steps.prompt: Can a $2 \times 3$ matrix ever equal a $3 \times 2$ matrix?
options: Only if both are zero matrices
steps.explanation: Equality requires the same shape before entries are even compared. Different dimensions end the conversation.

steps.title: Say the shape out loud
steps.body: Before any matrix operation, name each matrix's shape ("2 by 3"). Almost every matrix error — especially in multiplication, coming soon — is a shape error caught too late.

steps.title: The name predates the tool
steps.body: James Sylvester coined 'matrix' in 1850 — Latin for 'womb' — because he saw the grid as something determinants are born FROM. His friend Cayley then built the algebra you're about to learn.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Dimension = rows × columns, rows first; $a_{ij}$ = row $i$, column $j$.
Equality: same shape, every entry matching — a system in disguise.
$O$ and $I$ are the grid world's 0 and 1.

lessons.title: Adding & Scaling Matrices
lessons.concreteComparison: Two weeks of sales sheets? Add them cell by cell for the fortnight totals. Prices rising 10%? Multiply the whole price grid by 1.1. Matrix addition and scalar multiplication are exactly the spreadsheet moves you'd invent yourself — entrywise, no surprises.
lessons.objective: Add, subtract, and scale matrices entrywise, and solve simple matrix equations.
concept: **Addition** is entrywise, and only between matrices of the SAME shape: $(A + B)_{ij} = a_{ij} + b_{ij}$. Subtraction likewise. Different shapes simply don't add — there's no cell to pair.
**Scalar multiplication** hits every entry: $(kA)_{ij} = k \, a_{ij}$. Together with addition it builds combinations like $2A - 3B$, computed entry by entry.

The comfortable laws hold — $A + B = B + A$, $k(A + B) = kA + kB$ — so matrix equations at this level solve exactly like vector ones: peel operations, finish entrywise. (The one law that will NOT survive next lesson is commutativity of multiplication. Enjoy order-independence while it lasts.)
lessons.keyIdea: Add and scale entrywise, same-shape only; 2A − 3B and matrix equations work exactly like the vector versions.

facts.title: Addition
facts.explanation: Cell by cell — shapes must match.

facts.title: Familiar laws
facts.latex: A + B = B + A,\quad k(A + B) = kA + kB

commonMistakes.correction: A 2×2 plus a 2×3 is undefined — some cells have no partner. Check shapes before touching entries.
kA multiplies EVERY entry. Half-scaled matrices are the grid version of the half-scaled vector error — and just as fatal.

steps.title: Spreadsheet arithmetic
steps.body: Two sales sheets add cell by cell; a 10% price rise scales every cell. Matrix addition and scalar multiplication are those moves, formalized: $(A+B)_{ij} = a_{ij} + b_{ij}$ and $(kA)_{ij} = k\,a_{ij}$ — same-shape matrices only, every entry included.

steps.title: Everything familiar still works (for now)
steps.body: $A + B = B + A$, $k(A+B) = kA + kB$, and matrix equations peel like vector ones: $2X + B = C$ gives $X = \tfrac{1}{2}(C - B)$. Savor the comfort — next lesson, multiplication arrives and ORDER starts to matter.
beats: solve matrix equations by peeling, then finish entrywise

steps.title: One entry
steps.prompt: In $2\begin{pmatrix} 3 & -2 \\ 1 & 5 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$, the entry in row 1, column 2 is…
steps.explanation: $2 \cdot (-2) + 4 = 0$. Each cell computes independently — you never need the whole matrix to answer about one entry.

steps.title: Shapes first
steps.prompt: $A$ is $2 \times 3$ and $B$ is $3 \times 2$. What is $A + B$?
steps.explanation: Addition pairs cells; different shapes leave cells partnerless. (Multiplication is another story — next lesson.)

steps.title: One target entry? Compute one entry
steps.body: When a question asks for a single entry of 3A − 2B, don't build both scaled matrices — run the combination on that one cell. Faster, and fewer places to slip.

steps.title: Image filters are matrix sums
steps.body: Blending two photos is literally αA + (1−α)B on their pixel matrices — the fade sliders in every photo app are running this lesson at 60 frames a second.

steps.title: What sticks
points: $kA$ scales every entry; combinations compute cell by cell.
Matrix equations peel like vector equations.

lessons.title: Matrix Multiplication
lessons.concreteComparison: You already own the engine: matrix × point from the transformations world was row-times-column, twice. Full matrix multiplication is the same handshake repeated for EVERY row-column pair — the entry $(AB)_{ij}$ is row $i$ of $A$ dotted with column $j$ of $B$. It's dot products all the way down.
lessons.objective: Multiply matrices via row·column dot products, check dimension compatibility, and verify that order matters (AB ≠ BA).
concept: The recipe: $$(AB)_{ij} = \text{(row } i \text{ of } A) \cdot \text{(column } j \text{ of } B)$$ — a dot product for every cell of the answer. For $2\times 2$'s that's four dot products.

Shapes must SHAKE HANDS: $(m \times n)(n \times p) = m \times p$ — inner dimensions must match and then they cancel, leaving the outer pair. A $2\times 3$ times a $3\times 2$ is $2 \times 2$; reversed, it's $3 \times 3$: different animals entirely.

And the headline warning: **matrix multiplication is not commutative**. $AB \ne BA$ in general — even when both products exist and have the same shape. Order encodes which transformation happens first, and geometry cares about order (rotate-then-reflect ≠ reflect-then-rotate).
lessons.keyIdea: (AB)_ij = row i · column j; shapes (m×n)(n×p) = m×p; and AB ≠ BA — order is information.

facts.title: The recipe
facts.explanation: Row i of A dotted with column j of B — one dot product per answer cell.

facts.title: Handshake rule
facts.explanation: Inner dimensions match and cancel; outer pair survives.

facts.title: No commuting
facts.explanation: Order encodes which operation acts first.

workedExamples.solution: Four dot products: top-left $1\cdot5 + 2\cdot7 = 19$, top-right $1\cdot6 + 2\cdot8 = 22$, bottom-left $3\cdot5 + 4\cdot7 = 43$, bottom-right $3\cdot6 + 4\cdot8 = 50$. $AB = \begin{pmatrix} 19 & 22 \\ 43 & 50 \end{pmatrix}$.

workedExamples.statement: Same $A, B$: compute $BA$ and compare with $AB$.

workedExamples.solution: Two dot products: $\binom{2\cdot4 + (-1)\cdot5}{0\cdot4 + 3\cdot5} = \binom{3}{15}$. Matrix-times-point from the transformations chapter is exactly this, with a $2\times1$ partner.

commonMistakes.correction: (AB)₁₁ is NOT a₁₁b₁₁. It's the whole first row of A dotted with the whole first column of B. Multiplication is dot products, not cell-pairing.
Almost never true. If you silently swapped an order to 'simplify', you changed the answer — and in geometry, the transformation.

tryIt.statement: $A$ is $2 \times 3$, $B$ is $3 \times 4$. What is the shape of $AB$? Does $BA$ exist?
tryIt.solution: $AB$ is $2 \times 4$ (inner 3's shake hands). $BA$ would need $4 = 2$ — it doesn't exist.

steps.title: Dot products all the way down
steps.body: You've multiplied a matrix into a point: row·column, twice. The full product just repeats that handshake for every pair: $(AB)_{ij}$ = (row $i$ of $A$) · (column $j$ of $B$). A $2\times2$ product is four little dot products — organized, not hard.

steps.title: Shapes shake hands; order is sacred
steps.body: $(m \times n)(n \times p) = m \times p$: inner dimensions must MATCH, then they cancel. And the big one: $AB \ne BA$ in general. Order encodes which operation acts first — rotate-then-reflect is a different journey than reflect-then-rotate, and the algebra remembers.

steps.title: Handshake
steps.prompt: $P$ is $3 \times 2$ and $Q$ is $2 \times 5$. The product $PQ$ is…
steps.explanation: Inner 2's shake hands and cancel; the outer $3$ and $5$ survive: $3 \times 5$.

steps.title: Finger the row, finger the column
steps.body: Physically track: left finger sweeps A's row, right finger sweeps B's column, multiply as they walk. Mechanical — and it makes skipping a term nearly impossible.

steps.title: The trillion-dollar operation
steps.body: Neural networks are, computationally, towers of matrix multiplications. The world's AI chips are judged by one number: how many of THIS LESSON's operations they do per second.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Shapes: inner dimensions match and cancel.

lessons.title: The Identity & Matrix Powers
lessons.concreteComparison: Multiplying a number by 1 changes nothing. The grid world's 1 is the identity matrix $I$ — ones down the diagonal, zeros elsewhere: $AI = IA = A$, always. And once multiplying-by-itself makes sense, so do powers: $A^2 = AA$, with patterns exams love to hide inside.
lessons.objective: Use the identity matrix, compute A² and A³, and exploit patterns in matrix powers.
concept: The **identity** $I = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ is multiplication's do-nothing: $AI = IA = A$ for every compatible $A$. Its columns are the unit points $(1,0)$ and $(0,1)$ — the transformation that moves nothing.
**Powers** stack copies: $A^2 = AA$, $A^3 = A^2A$. Since a matrix always commutes with ITSELF, powers behave: $A^m A^n = A^{m+n}$. (It's mixing different matrices that breaks order.)

Exam patterns: diagonal matrices power entrywise — $\begin{pmatrix} a & 0 \\ 0 & b \end{pmatrix}^n = \begin{pmatrix} a^n & 0 \\ 0 & b^n \end{pmatrix}$; some matrices CYCLE (a 90° rotation returns to $I$ in four steps: $R^4 = I$); and some die — nonzero matrices with $A^2 = O$ exist, a purely matrix phenomenon numbers never allowed.
lessons.keyIdea: I is the multiplicative 1 (AI = IA = A); powers stack copies and commute with themselves; diagonals power entrywise, rotations cycle.

facts.title: Identity
facts.explanation: The do-nothing transformation; the grid world's 1.

facts.title: Diagonal powers
facts.explanation: Diagonals are the easy lane — entries power independently.

facts.title: Cycles
facts.explanation: Four quarter-turns come home; powers of rotations repeat with period 4.

workedExamples.statement: Compute $A^2$ for $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$, then guess and verify $A^3$.
workedExamples.solution: $A^2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$; the corner counts the copies. Indeed $A^3 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ — in general $A^n$ has $n$ in the corner.

commonMistakes.correction: A² means A TIMES A — full row-column products. Only diagonal matrices get the entrywise shortcut.
The middle term is AB + BA, and they differ in general. The binomial formula needs commuting — matrices usually refuse.

tryIt.solution: Row·column: every entry of $A^2$ is $0$ — a nonzero matrix whose square is zero. Numbers never do this.

steps.title: The matrix that does nothing
steps.body: $I = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ fixes every point — its columns are just $(1,0)$ and $(0,1)$ staying home. Algebraically it's the multiplicative 1: $AI = IA = A$, the rare product where order doesn't matter.

steps.title: Stack copies, hunt patterns
steps.body: $A^2 = AA$, $A^3 = A^2A$ — and a matrix commutes with itself, so $A^mA^n = A^{m+n}$ safely. The exam game is pattern-hunting: diagonals power entrywise, the 90° rotation cycles with $R^4 = I$, and huge exponents collapse via remainders.
beats: rotations cycle: R⁴ = I — use remainders mod 4

steps.title: Multiply by I
steps.explanation: The identity changes nothing — check any cell: row 1 of $I$ · column 1 gives $1 \cdot 7 + 0 \cdot 3 = 7$.

steps.title: Ride the cycle
steps.prompt: $R$ is the 90° rotation matrix, $R^4 = I$. Then $R^{101} = $ ?
steps.explanation: $101 = 4 \cdot 25 + 1$, so $R^{101} = (R^4)^{25}R = R$. Big exponents die by remainder.

steps.title: Compute A² before dreaming
steps.body: Every 'find A^big' question starts the same way: compute A² honestly and LOOK at it. Is it diagonal? A multiple of I? A? The pattern announces itself in one multiplication.

steps.title: Fibonacci lives in a matrix
steps.body: Power up $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ and the Fibonacci numbers appear in the entries — $A^n$ holds $F_{n+1}, F_n, F_{n-1}$. Fast matrix powering is how computers get the trillionth Fibonacci number in microseconds.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Diagonals power entrywise; rotations cycle ($R^4 = I$).
$(A+B)^2 \ne A^2 + 2AB + B^2$ — commuting is not free.
